title: Metrics Board
subtitle: Interactive On-Demand Market Analysis Panel — 6 Analysis Buttons, H1 Timeframe
author: Christian Benjamin (lynnchris)
verdict: REFERENCE — extract the 6 metrics as a structured data object. The UI architecture is MQL5-specific but the metric set is reusable.

core_concept.name: On-Demand Metrics Retrieval
core_concept.description: Display only what the trader asks for, when they ask for it. Six dedicated analysis views, each fetched on button click. Reduces cognitive load compared to always-on dashboards.

dialog_x.description: Panel left edge (pixels from chart left)

dialog_y.description: Panel top edge (pixels from chart top)

metrics_specification.note: This is the key reusable output of Part 8 — the 6 metrics as a structured data contract

contract.trend_ma14: float — 14-period SMA of close prices on H1
contract.sma_50: float — 50-period SMA of close prices on H1

metrics_specification.python_dataclass: MetricsSnapshot(h1_high, h1_low, h1_volume, trend_ma14, volatility_atr14, sma_50, support, resistance, timestamp)

steps.name: init_ui
steps.description: Build CAppDialog with 6 analysis buttons + 1 close button

steps.name: wait_for_click
steps.description: OnChartEvent() listens for button click events

steps.name: route_event
steps.description: Match button name to analysis method: 'btnHighLow' → PerformHighLowAnalysis()

steps.name: fetch_and_display
steps.description: Each analysis method fetches data and updates results label

keep: The 6 metric categories — these are the fundamental on-chart data points every trader needs
MetricsSnapshot data contract — clean structured object, reusable across many tools
On-demand retrieval pattern — fetch when needed, not on every tick
Previous bar H/L as S/R — same as Parts 1-2, validates this as standard practice
discard: CAppDialog / CButton MQL5 GUI framework — replaced by Python dashboard (Streamlit/Tkinter/web)
H1 hardcoded timeframe — make configurable, support multiple TFs
Button-click event model — replaced by REST endpoint or Python function call
MQL5 iHigh(), iLow(), iVolume() calls — replaced by BrokerDataAdapter
improve: Add multi-symbol support — run MetricsCalculator across all symbols in one call
Add historical comparison — current ATR vs 5-day average ATR
Add timeframe selection — not just H1
Add export to JSON/CSV for downstream tools
Integrate with NotificationService — alert when ATR spikes or S/R breached

creviaderiv_insights.h1_as_macro_context: Using H1 for High/Low, Volume, and S/R matches our approach of using higher-timeframe context. H1 S/R (prev bar H/L) is functionally similar to our Zone boundaries from M15.
creviaderiv_insights.atr_for_volatility: ATR(14) on H1 is the standard volatility metric. We use ATR(14) on M15 for SL floor calculation. Same tool, appropriate timeframe for the purpose.
creviaderiv_insights.sma_50_trend_filter: 50-period SMA on H1 for trend direction — similar to how we use market_structure (BULLISH/BEARISH) from StructureEngine. The SMA gives a simpler directional read.

article_figures.title: EA Logic Summary Diagram
article_figures.insight: Architecture diagram showing 6 analysis modules feeding the results panel. Clean separation of concerns.

article_figures.title: Library Inclusion GIF
article_figures.insight: Shows MQL5 StandardLibrary Dialog.mqh / Button.mqh includes needed for the UI. Irrelevant for Python but confirms MQL5 UI requires external libraries.

article_figures.title: Functionality Demonstration (GIF)
article_figures.insight: Animated panel showing each button click → result display cycle. Confirms on-demand model and result string format.

article_figures.title: Expert Log Output
article_figures.insight: Shows all button click events logged to MT5 Experts tab. Confirms logging pattern for debugging.
